examples.explanation:
Start at col=0, board width N=3
1. Try vertical at col=0: Remaining board 2×2 (cols 1-2)
   • At col=1: Try vertical → remaining 2×1 (col 2) → vertical at col=2 → complete (count+1)
   • At col=1: Try horizontal → needs col=2 → place both → complete (count+1)
2. Try horizontal at col=0: Needs col=1 → place both, remaining 2×1 (col 2)
   • Only vertical possible at col=2 → complete (count+1)
Total count = 3

Start at col=0, N=4
1. Vertical at col=0 → remaining 2×3 → recursively:
   • For 2×3: vertical at col=1 → remaining 2×2 → 2 ways (as in N=2)
   • For 2×3: horizontal at col=1 → needs col=2 → remaining 2×1 → 1 way
   Total for 2×3 = 2+1 = 3 ways
2. Horizontal at col=0 → needs col=1 → remaining 2×2 → 2 ways (as in N=2)
Total = 3 + 2 = 5

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^N)
approaches.summary: Recursively try all placements of dominoes (vertical or horizontal) to cover the 2xN board, counting valid tilings. This is straightforward but inefficient for large N due to exponential growth.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Ways to Tile 2×N Board Using Brute Force Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution exploring all possible domino placements to count valid tilings of a 2×N board.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force recursive solution to count all ways to tile a 2×N board using dominoes (2×1 vertical or 1×2 horizontal pairs). The intuition is to treat the board as a sequence of columns from left to right, and at each step, we have two choices: place a vertical domino covering the current column (if available), or place two horizontal dominoes covering the current and next column (if both columns are available). We recursively explore all possibilities until the board is completely covered, counting each valid complete tiling. The algorithm starts at column position 0 and recursively tries both placement options. When we reach column N, we've covered the entire board and count it as one valid tiling. The time complexity is O(2^N) because at each column we have up to 2 choices, leading to exponential growth. Space complexity is O(N) for the recursion stack depth. This connects directly to the key subroutine (L1) which is the recursive function itself. Common mistakes include forgetting to check bounds when placing horizontal dominoes, not handling the base case correctly, and double-counting arrangements due to incorrect state representation. The recursive nature makes this approach intuitive but impractical for large N due to exponential time complexity.

examples.trace:
Start at col=0, board width N=3
1. Try vertical at col=0: Remaining board 2×2 (cols 1-2)
   • At col=1: Try vertical → remaining 2×1 (col 2) → vertical at col=2 → complete (count+1)
   • At col=1: Try horizontal → needs col=2 → place both → complete (count+1)
2. Try horizontal at col=0: Needs col=1 → place both, remaining 2×1 (col 2)
   • Only vertical possible at col=2 → complete (count+1)
Total count = 3

Start at col=0, N=4
1. Vertical at col=0 → remaining 2×3 → recursively:
   • For 2×3: vertical at col=1 → remaining 2×2 → 2 ways (as in N=2)
   • For 2×3: horizontal at col=1 → needs col=2 → remaining 2×1 → 1 way
   Total for 2×3 = 2+1 = 3 ways
2. Horizontal at col=0 → needs col=1 → remaining 2×2 → 2 ways (as in N=2)
Total = 3 + 2 = 5

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Function to Explore Domino Placements
ladders.desc: The recursive function that tries placing vertical or horizontal dominoes from a given column position.
ladders.explanation: This is the core recursive function that implements the brute force exploration. Given a current column position 'col' and total board width N, the function returns the number of ways to tile the remaining 2×(N-col) board. The intuition is that at each step, we consider the first uncovered column and make a decision that affects subsequent columns. The algorithm has two main cases: 1) Base case: if col == N, we've covered all columns, return 1 (one complete tiling). 2) Recursive case: try placing a vertical domino covering the current column (if col < N), which moves us to col+1, plus try placing two horizontal dominoes covering columns col and col+1 (if col+1 < N), which moves us to col+2. The sum of results from both attempts gives total ways. Time complexity for this function on its own is O(2^(N-col)) in the worst case. Space complexity is O(N-col) for recursion depth. This function is exactly what's called in L0 to solve the full problem. Common mistakes include incorrect base case handling (should return 1, not 0), not checking bounds for horizontal placement, and forgetting to sum results from both options. The function embodies the divide-and-conquer strategy of breaking the problem into smaller subproblems.

examples.trace:
col=0, N=2 (remaining 2×2)
1. Try vertical: call with col=1, N=2
   • col=1, N=2: Try vertical → col=2 → base case return 1
   • col=1, N=2: Try horizontal → needs col=2 (invalid, col+1=2 not < N=2) → skip
   → returns 1
2. Try horizontal: needs col=1 (valid, 1 < 2) → call with col=2, N=2
   • col=2 → base case return 1
Total = 1 + 1 = 2

col=1, N=3 (remaining 2×2)
1. Try vertical: call with col=2, N=3
   • col=2, N=3: Try vertical → col=3 → base case return 1
   • col=2, N=3: Try horizontal → needs col=3 (invalid, 3 not < 3) → skip
   → returns 1
2. Try horizontal: needs col=2 (valid, 2 < 3) → call with col=3, N=3
   • col=3 → base case return 1
Total = 1 + 1 = 2

ladders.title: Core Logic — Decision Tree for Domino Placement Choices
ladders.desc: The branching logic that determines whether to place a vertical or horizontal domino at a given position.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the decision-making logic at a single step: given we're at column 'col' with N total columns, how do we decide what placements are possible? The intuition is that we have exactly two mutually exclusive options that cover the leftmost uncovered cells. A vertical domino (2×1) covers both cells in the current column, advancing us by 1 column. Two horizontal dominoes (each 1×2) placed as a pair cover two adjacent columns, advancing us by 2 columns. The logic must check: 1) Vertical is always possible if col < N (since we have at least one column left). 2) Horizontal is possible only if col+1 < N (since we need two consecutive columns available). This creates a binary decision tree where each node represents a state (current column), and edges represent placement choices. Time complexity for exploring this logic once is O(1). Space is O(1). This logic is the heart of the recursive function in L1. Common mistakes include allowing horizontal placement when only one column remains, or incorrectly assuming other placement patterns (like single horizontal domino, which would leave gaps). The key insight is that these two options are exhaustive for covering the board without gaps when proceeding left-to-right.

examples.trace:
At col=0, N=3:
1. Check vertical: col < N? 0 < 3 → true → possible
2. Check horizontal: col+1 < N? 0+1=1 < 3 → true → possible
Result: Both options available

At col=2, N=3:
1. Check vertical: col < N? 2 < 3 → true → possible
2. Check horizontal: col+1 < N? 2+1=3 < 3 → false → not possible
Result: Only vertical option available

ladders.title: Building Block — Representing Board State as Column Index
ladders.desc: How to represent the tiling progress using a simple integer column position.
ladders.explanation: This level addresses how we represent the state of a partially tiled board. The intuition is that since we're tiling left-to-right and dominoes cover columns completely, we only need to track how many columns have been fully covered so far. We can represent the state with a single integer 'col' indicating that columns 0 through col-1 are completely tiled, and we're now at column 'col' ready to place the next domino. This representation works because: 1) Dominoes don't overlap, 2) We always fill from left to right without skipping columns, 3) Each placement covers entire column(s). The algorithm uses this representation to make decisions: if col == N, board is fully tiled; otherwise, we place dominoes starting at col. Time and space for maintaining this state are O(1). This feeds into L2's decision logic which uses col to determine possible moves. Common mistakes include using more complex state representations (like tracking individual cells), which complicates the logic unnecessarily, or incorrectly updating col (e.g., adding wrong increments). The simplicity of this representation is key to the recursive approach's elegance.

examples.trace:
Board: 2×3, all cells empty
Representation: col=0 (no columns covered yet, start at column 0)
Visual: Columns: [0 empty, 1 empty, 2 empty]

examples.input: After placing vertical domino at col=0 on N=3
examples.trace:
Start: col=0
Place vertical domino covering column 0
Now columns 0 is fully covered
New state: col=1 (next to cover column 1)
Visual: Columns: [0 covered, 1 empty, 2 empty]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Placing a Single Domino and Updating Position
ladders.desc: The atomic action of placing one domino (vertical or horizontal pair) and advancing the column pointer.
ladders.explanation: This level breaks down the fundamental operation: placing a domino and updating our position. For a vertical domino (2×1), we cover both cells in the current column, so we advance by 1 column: new_col = col + 1. For two horizontal dominoes (effectively a 2×2 block covering two adjacent columns), we cover the current and next column, so we advance by 2 columns: new_col = col + 2. The intuition is that each domino placement consumes a certain width of the board, moving our 'frontier' forward. The algorithm is simple: if placing vertical, return col+1; if placing horizontal, return col+2. Time and space are O(1). This operation is used repeatedly in L3's state transitions. Common mistakes include advancing by wrong amounts (e.g., +2 for vertical), or not verifying that the placement is valid (which is handled at L2). This operation assumes the placement is valid; validity checking is separate. Understanding this helps see how the recursive function progresses through the board.

examples.trace:
Current col=0
Place vertical domino covering column 0
Domino width = 1 column
New col = 0 + 1 = 1

Current col=1
Place two horizontal dominoes covering columns 1 and 2
Domino width = 2 columns
New col = 1 + 2 = 3

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding a 2×N Grid and Domino Shapes
ladders.desc: Visualizing the board as columns and dominoes as rectangles covering cells.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level introduces the basic elements: a 2×N board is a grid with 2 rows and N columns, totaling 2N cells. Dominoes are rectangles that cover exactly 2 cells. There are two orientations relevant to tiling: 1) Vertical domino: 2×1, covering both cells in a single column. 2) Horizontal domino: 1×2, covering one cell in each of two adjacent columns (but we always use them in pairs to cover both rows in those columns). The intuition is that to cover the board completely without gaps or overlaps, we need to combine these shapes. The algorithm at this level is simply to visualize or model the board as a 2D grid and dominoes as blocks that can be placed. Time and space are not applicable here as it's conceptual. This feeds into L4 where we start placing these dominoes. Common mistakes include misunderstanding dimensions (e.g., thinking domino is 1×2 instead of 2×1 for vertical), or not realizing horizontal dominoes must be used in pairs to cover both rows. This is essential for beginners to grasp before attempting any tiling logic.

examples.trace:
Visual representation:
Row 0: [cell00, cell01, cell02]
Row 1: [cell10, cell11, cell12]
Columns: col0 (cells 00,10), col1 (01,11), col2 (02,12)
Total cells: 6

On 2×3 board:
Vertical domino (2×1) placed at column 1:
Covers cell01 (row0, col1) and cell11 (row1, col1)
Remaining cells: all except those two

testCases.input: Vertical domino covers how many cells?
Two horizontal dominoes cover how many cells?
Can a single horizontal domino cover a 2×N board alone?
How many columns does a vertical domino cover?
How many columns do two horizontal dominoes cover?
Is a 2×1 board tileable with dominoes?

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Memoization O(N)
approaches.summary: Use recursion with memoization to store results for subproblems (tilings for smaller N), avoiding redundant calculations. This reduces time complexity by caching intermediate states.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Ways to Tile 2×N Using DP with Memoization
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting domino tilings for 2×N board using recursion with memoization.
ladders.explanation: This problem asks to count all possible ways to tile a 2×N board using 2×1 dominoes (which can be placed vertically or horizontally). The key insight is that tilings for N can be built from tilings for smaller N. When placing the first domino at the left end, we have two choices: place a vertical domino covering the first column (2×1), which leaves a 2×(N-1) board to tile, or place two horizontal dominoes stacked to cover the first two columns (2×2), which leaves a 2×(N-2) board to tile. This gives the recurrence: f(N) = f(N-1) + f(N-2), with base cases f(0)=1 (empty board has one way: do nothing) and f(1)=1 (only vertical placement). However, directly implementing this recurrence recursively leads to exponential time due to overlapping subproblems. We use memoization (top-down DP) to store computed f(k) values in a dictionary/array to avoid redundant calculations. The intuition is that the number of tilings follows the Fibonacci sequence, but here we're counting arrangements of dominoes on a grid. The algorithm: define a recursive function count(N) that returns f(N). If N is in memo, return it. Otherwise, if N==0 or N==1, return 1. Else, compute result = count(N-1) + count(N-2), store in memo, and return. Time complexity is O(N) because each f(k) is computed once. Space complexity is O(N) for memo storage and recursion stack. Connection to L1: This uses the recursive helper function with memoization as the key subroutine. Common mistakes: forgetting base cases, not handling N=0 correctly (should be 1, not 0), using recursion without memoization (exponential time), or misinterpreting the board dimensions. Real code pattern: def count_ways(N, memo={}): if N in memo: return memo[N]; if N <= 1: return 1; memo[N] = count_ways(N-1, memo) + count_ways(N-2, memo); return memo[N].

examples.trace: Call count_ways(3). Not in memo. N>1, so call count_ways(2) + count_ways(1). First, count_ways(2): not in memo, call count_ways(1) + count_ways(0). count_ways(1): base case, return 1. count_ways(0): base case, return 1. So count_ways(2) = 1+1 = 2, store memo[2]=2. Back to count_ways(3): count_ways(1) is base case, return 1. So count_ways(3) = 2 + 1 = 3, store memo[3]=3. Return 3.

Call count_ways(4). Not in memo. Call count_ways(3) + count_ways(2). count_ways(3): from memo (previous example) returns 3. count_ways(2): from memo returns 2. So count_ways(4) = 3 + 2 = 5, store memo[4]=5. Return 5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Function with Memoization for Fibonacci-like Sequence
ladders.desc: Implement a recursive function that computes f(N) = f(N-1) + f(N-2) using memoization.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the DP solution: a recursive function that computes values based on a recurrence relation while caching results to avoid recomputation. The function takes an integer N and a memo dictionary (initially empty) as parameters. It checks if N is already in memo; if yes, returns the cached value. Otherwise, it handles base cases: if N <= 1, returns 1 (since f(0)=1 and f(1)=1 for the tiling problem). For N > 1, it recursively calls itself for N-1 and N-2, sums the results, stores the sum in memo[N], and returns it. The intuition is that many recursive calls will be repeated (e.g., f(3) calls f(2) and f(1), and f(2) might be called multiple times), so memoization ensures each f(k) is computed only once. This transforms exponential time recursion into linear time. Step-by-step: 1. Define function f(N, memo). 2. If N in memo: return memo[N]. 3. If N <= 1: return 1. 4. Else: result = f(N-1, memo) + f(N-2, memo). 5. memo[N] = result. 6. Return result. Time complexity: O(N) because each f(k) for k=0..N is computed once. Space complexity: O(N) for memo storage and recursion stack depth. Connection to L0: This function is called by the main solution to compute the answer. Connection to L2: It relies on the recurrence relation f(N)=f(N-1)+f(N-2). Common mistakes: not passing memo dictionary correctly (should be mutable and shared across calls), infinite recursion if base cases are wrong, or not storing results before returning. Real code pattern: def fib_memo(N, memo={}): if N in memo: return memo[N]; if N <= 1: return 1; memo[N] = fib_memo(N-1, memo) + fib_memo(N-2, memo); return memo[N].

examples.trace: Call fib_memo(2, memo={}). Not in memo. N>1, so call fib_memo(1, memo) + fib_memo(0, memo). First, fib_memo(1): base case, return 1. fib_memo(0): base case, return 1. Sum = 2. Store memo[2]=2. Return 2.

Call fib_memo(3, memo={}). Not in memo. Call fib_memo(2, memo) + fib_memo(1, memo). fib_memo(2): as above, returns 2 (and memo now has {2:2}). fib_memo(1): base case, return 1. Sum = 3. Store memo[3]=3. Return 3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recurrence Relation f(N) = f(N-1) + f(N-2) for Domino Tilings
ladders.desc: Derive and apply the recurrence relation that defines the number of tilings.
ladders.explanation: The central insight for counting domino tilings on a 2×N board is the recurrence relation: f(N) = f(N-1) + f(N-2), where f(N) represents the number of ways to tile a 2×N board. To derive this, consider the first column (leftmost) of the board. We have two choices for placing dominoes: 1. Place a vertical domino (2×1) covering the entire first column. This uses up one column, leaving a 2×(N-1) board to tile, which can be done in f(N-1) ways. 2. Place two horizontal dominoes stacked on top of each other, covering the first two columns (since each horizontal domino is 1×2, but placed vertically they span 2 rows and 1 column? Wait, correction: A horizontal domino is 1×2, so to cover width 2, we need two horizontal dominoes placed side-by-side horizontally? Actually, in standard domino tiling, dominoes are 2×1 or 1×2. For a 2×N board, if we place a horizontal domino, it covers 1 row and 2 columns, but since the board has 2 rows, we must place two horizontal dominoes stacked vertically to cover both rows across the same two columns. So, placing two horizontal dominoes (one in top row, one in bottom row) covers a 2×2 area, leaving a 2×(N-2) board to tile, which can be done in f(N-2) ways. These are the only possibilities because dominoes cannot overlap or extend beyond the board. Thus, total ways f(N) = f(N-1) + f(N-2). Base cases: f(0)=1 (empty board, one way to do nothing), f(1)=1 (only vertical placement possible). This recurrence is identical to the Fibonacci sequence but with different base values. The intuition is that tilings decompose based on the first placement. Step-by-step: To compute f(N), if N=0 or 1, return 1. Otherwise, compute f(N) as sum of f(N-1) and f(N-2). This can be implemented recursively or iteratively. Time complexity if implemented naively recursively is exponential O(2^N), but with memoization or iteration it becomes O(N). Space complexity for iterative DP is O(1) if only last two values are stored. Connection to L1: This recurrence is computed by the recursive function. Connection to L3: It relies on understanding board dimensions and domino placements. Common mistakes: Incorrectly deriving recurrence (e.g., missing the horizontal case or mis counting columns), or wrong base cases. Real code pattern: For iterative: if N<=1: return 1; a,b=1,1; for i in range(2,N+1): a,b = b, a+b; return b.

examples.trace: f(2) = f(1) + f(0) = 1 + 1 = 2. This matches: ways to tile 2×2: either two vertical dominoes or two horizontal dominoes stacked.
f(3) = f(2) + f(1) = 2 + 1 = 3. Ways: VVV, VHH, HHV (where V=vertical, H=two horizontals stacked covering 2 columns).

ladders.title: Building Block — Understanding Board Dimensions and Domino Placements
ladders.desc: Analyze how dominoes (2×1 or 1×2) can be arranged on a 2×N grid.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves visualizing a 2×N board and the possible placements of dominoes. A standard domino tile covers two adjacent cells: it can be oriented vertically (2×1, covering two cells in the same column across two rows) or horizontally (1×2, covering two cells in the same row across two columns). For a 2×N board, which has 2 rows and N columns, we need to cover all 2N cells with dominoes without gaps or overlaps. Key observations: 1. Vertical domino: Placed in a single column, covering both rows. This reduces the problem to tiling a 2×(N-1) board. 2. Horizontal domino: Since the board has 2 rows, a horizontal domino covers 1 row and 2 columns. To cover both rows in the same two columns, we must place two horizontal dominoes stacked vertically (one in top row, one in bottom row), effectively covering a 2×2 block. This reduces the problem to tiling a 2×(N-2) board. There are no other possibilities because dominoes cannot be placed diagonally or in other orientations. The intuition is that tilings are built incrementally from the left edge. Step-by-step: To tile a 2×N board, start at the leftmost column. Option A: Place a vertical domino, cover column 1, move to column 2. Option B: Place two horizontal dominoes (stacked), cover columns 1 and 2, move to column 3. This decomposition ensures all tilings are counted exactly once. This building block is crucial for deriving the recurrence relation. Time/space analysis: This is a conceptual step, but understanding it is O(1) in terms of computation. Connection to L2: This analysis leads to the recurrence f(N)=f(N-1)+f(N-2). Connection to L4: It relies on counting cells and dimensions. Common mistakes: Confusing domino orientations, thinking horizontal domino covers 2×1 area (it's 1×2), or missing that two horizontals are needed for the 2×2 block. Real code pattern: Not directly coded, but used in reasoning: def possible_placements(N): if N==0: return 1; if N==1: return 1 (vertical only); else: return placements(N-1) + placements(N-2).

examples.trace: Board is 2×1. Only placement: one vertical domino covering the single column. No horizontal possible because need 2 columns for horizontal.
Board is 2×2. Way 1: Two vertical dominoes (one in each column). Way 2: Two horizontal dominoes stacked (covering first two columns in top row and bottom row).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Summing Two Numbers and Storing in a Dictionary
ladders.desc: Perform addition and cache the result in a key-value store.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves computing the sum of two numbers and storing the result in a dictionary (or hash map) for later retrieval. In the context of memoization, we use a dictionary where keys are subproblem identifiers (e.g., N values) and values are the computed results (e.g., number of tilings). The steps are: 1. Retrieve two values, typically from recursive calls or previous computations. 2. Add them together to get a sum. 3. Store this sum in the dictionary under a specific key. 4. Return the sum. The intuition is that by caching results, we avoid redundant calculations: if the same subproblem is encountered again, we simply look up the value in the dictionary instead of recomputing. This is a key technique in dynamic programming. Step-by-step algorithm: Given a dictionary memo, a key k, and values a and b: result = a + b; memo[k] = result; return result. In pseudocode: if k not in memo: memo[k] = compute_value(k); return memo[k]. Time complexity: O(1) for addition and dictionary storage/retrieval on average. Space complexity: O(1) per storage, but overall O(N) for storing N results. Connection to L3: This operation is used when computing f(N) from f(N-1) and f(N-2). Connection to L5: It relies on basic arithmetic and data structures. Common mistakes: Forgetting to store the result, using wrong key, or not initializing the dictionary properly. Real code pattern: memo = {}; def compute(k): if k in memo: return memo[k]; # compute a and b somehow; result = a + b; memo[k] = result; return result.

examples.input: Compute sum for key=2 with a=1, b=1
examples.trace: Start with empty memo={}. Compute result = 1 + 1 = 2. Store memo[2] = 2. Return 2. Now memo is {2:2}.

examples.input: Compute sum for key=3 with a=2 (from memo), b=1
examples.trace: Memo is {2:2}. Compute result = 2 + 1 = 3. Store memo[3] = 3. Return 3. Memo becomes {2:2, 3:3}.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Recursion and Base Cases
ladders.desc: Understand recursive function calls and terminating conditions.
ladders.explanation: Recursion is a programming technique where a function calls itself to solve smaller instances of the same problem. A recursive function must have: 1. Base case(s): Simple, non-recursive cases that stop the recursion (e.g., when the problem size is zero or one). 2. Recursive case: Where the function calls itself with a modified argument (e.g., reducing the problem size). The intuition is to break down a complex problem into simpler subproblems until reaching a base case, then combine results as the recursion unwinds. For example, to compute f(N), we might compute f(N-1) and f(N-2) recursively. Step-by-step: Define a function f(n). If n meets a base condition (e.g., n == 0 or n == 1), return a predefined value (e.g., 1). Otherwise, return f(n-1) + f(n-2). This mirrors mathematical recurrence relations. Time complexity without optimization can be exponential due to repeated calculations, but it introduces the concept of solving via self-reference. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack depth. Connection to L4: Recursion is used to compute values that are then summed and stored. Connection to higher levels: This is the foundational idea for the DP approach. Common mistakes: Missing base cases (leading to infinite recursion), not reducing problem size in recursive calls, or misunderstanding how recursion unwinds. Real code pattern: def f(n): if n <= 1: return 1; return f(n-1) + f(n-2).

examples.trace: Call f(0). Base case n<=1 is true, so return 1. No recursive calls.
Call f(2). n>1, so recursive case: return f(1) + f(0). First, call f(1): base case, return 1. Then, call f(0): base case, return 1. Sum: 1+1=2. Return 2.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming Bottom-Up O(N)
approaches.summary: Iteratively build a DP array where dp[i] represents ways to tile 2xi, using recurrence dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]. This efficiently computes the Fibonacci-like sequence for tiling counts.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Ways to Tile 2×N Using Bottom-Up DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution to count domino tiling arrangements for 2×N board using iterative DP with O(N) time and space.
ladders.explanation: This problem asks for the number of distinct ways to tile a 2×N board using 2×1 dominoes (which can be placed vertically or horizontally). The key insight is recognizing this forms a Fibonacci-like sequence: dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]. For a 2×i board, consider the rightmost column. If we place a vertical domino covering both rows of the last column, we're left with a 2×(i-1) board (dp[i-1] ways). If we place two horizontal dominoes stacked to cover the last two columns, we're left with a 2×(i-2) board (dp[i-2] ways). These are the only possibilities since dominoes don't overlap. Base cases: dp[0]=1 (empty board, one way), dp[1]=1 (single vertical domino). The algorithm initializes dp[0] and dp[1], then iterates from 2 to N, computing dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]. Time complexity is O(N) due to the single loop; space is O(N) for the dp array, though it can be optimized to O(1) by keeping only the last two values. This connects to L1 by implementing the recurrence directly. Common mistakes include incorrect base cases (dp[0] should be 1, not 0) and off-by-one errors in indexing. Real code pattern: def countWays(N): if N <= 1: return 1; dp = [0]*(N+1); dp[0]=dp[1]=1; for i in range(2,N+1): dp[i]=dp[i-1]+dp[i-2]; return dp[N].

examples.trace: Initialize dp[0]=1, dp[1]=1. For i=2: dp[2]=dp[1]+dp[0]=1+1=2. For i=3: dp[3]=dp[2]+dp[1]=2+1=3. Return dp[3]=3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Compute Fibonacci-Like Recurrence for Tiling
ladders.desc: Implement the recurrence dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2] to build the sequence of tiling counts.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on computing the recurrence relation that defines the tiling counts. The intuition is that for any board length i, the number of tilings depends only on the counts for lengths i-1 and i-2, due to the placement options at the end. The algorithm takes an integer N and returns the N-th term in this sequence, starting from dp[0]=1. It uses a DP array of size N+1, initializes the first two terms, then fills the rest using the recurrence. Time complexity is O(N) for the loop; space is O(N) for the array. This feeds into L0 by providing the core computation needed for the full problem. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle N=0 or N=1 separately, or using incorrect indices. Real code pattern: def computeSequence(N): if N<0: return 0; dp=[0]*(N+1); dp[0]=1; if N>=1: dp[1]=1; for i in range(2,N+1): dp[i]=dp[i-1]+dp[i-2]; return dp[N].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Derive Recurrence Relation for Tiling Counts
ladders.desc: Understand and derive the recurrence dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2] by analyzing placement possibilities.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the central insight: why the tiling count follows this recurrence. Consider a 2×i board. Look at the rightmost column. There are two mutually exclusive ways to place dominoes ending at this column. First, place a vertical domino covering both rows of the last column. This uses up one column, leaving a 2×(i-1) board, which has dp[i-1] tilings. Second, place two horizontal dominoes stacked to cover the last two columns (since horizontal dominoes are 1×2, they span two columns). This uses up two columns, leaving a 2×(i-2) board, with dp[i-2] tilings. No other configurations are possible because dominoes must align without gaps. Thus, total ways dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]. This is a Fibonacci sequence shifted: dp[0]=1, dp[1]=1. The algorithm involves reasoning through these cases rather than coding. Time/space analysis is conceptual here. This connects to L1 by providing the logic for the recurrence computation. Common mistakes include missing the horizontal case or not justifying why these are the only options. Real code pattern is not applicable here; it's about derivation.

examples.trace: For 2×2 board: Option 1: vertical domino at last column → leaves 2×1 board (dp[1]=1 way). Option 2: two horizontal dominoes stacked at last two columns → leaves 2×0 board (dp[0]=1 way). Total: 1+1=2 ways.
For 2×3 board: Option 1: vertical at last column → leaves 2×2 board (dp[2]=2 ways). Option 2: horizontals at last two columns → leaves 2×1 board (dp[1]=1 way). Total: 2+1=3 ways.

ladders.title: Building Block — Initialize and Iterate Over a DP Array
ladders.desc: Set up a dynamic programming array and fill it iteratively using previous values.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves creating and populating a DP array to solve problems with overlapping subproblems. The intuition is that by storing results of smaller subproblems, we avoid redundant computations. For this tiling problem, we use an array dp where dp[i] stores the number of ways to tile 2×i. We initialize dp[0] and dp[1] as base cases. Then, we iterate from i=2 to N, computing each dp[i] based on dp[i-1] and dp[i-2]. This ensures each value is computed once and reused. Time complexity is O(N) for the loop; space is O(N) for the array. This feeds into L2 by providing the mechanism to apply the recurrence. Common mistakes include incorrect array sizing (need N+1 elements) or off-by-one in the loop range. Real code pattern: dp = [0]*(N+1); dp[0]=1; dp[1]=1; for i in range(2, N+1): dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Sum Two Previous Array Elements
ladders.desc: Perform the addition operation dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2] within a loop.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves accessing two previous elements in an array and summing them to compute a new value. The intuition is that in sequences defined by recurrences, each term depends on earlier terms. Here, for index i, we look at the values at positions i-1 and i-2, add them, and store the result at i. This is done in a loop to build the sequence iteratively. Time complexity per operation is O(1); overall, for N iterations, it's O(N). Space is O(1) per operation, but overall depends on array storage. This feeds into L3 by being the core step in filling the DP array. Common mistakes include accessing indices out of bounds (e.g., i-2 when i=1) or using wrong variable names. Real code pattern: for i in range(2, N+1): dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2].

examples.trace: Given dp array initially [1,1,0,0]. For i=2: access dp[1]=1 and dp[0]=1. Sum: 1+1=2. Store at dp[2] → array becomes [1,1,2,0].
Given dp array [1,1,2,0]. For i=3: access dp[2]=2 and dp[1]=1. Sum: 2+1=3. Store at dp[3] → array becomes [1,1,2,3].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand Array Indexing and Loops
ladders.desc: Learn basic array operations and loop constructs to traverse and update elements.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: working with arrays and loops. An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, accessible by indices starting from 0. For example, an array dp of size M has indices 0 to M-1. A loop, such as a for loop, allows repeating operations multiple times. In this context, we use a loop to iterate over indices from a start to an end, performing operations like accessing array elements at specific indices (e.g., dp[i-1]) and updating them (e.g., dp[i] = value). The intuition is that by systematically processing each index, we can build up results step by step. Time complexity depends on the number of iterations; space depends on array size. This feeds into L4 by providing the basic skills to implement the summation operation. Common mistakes include index errors (e.g., accessing dp[-1] or dp[M]) or infinite loops. Real code pattern: dp = [0]*5; dp[0]=1; for i in range(1,5): dp[i] = i*2.

examples.input: Initialize array of size 3 with zeros, set dp[0]=1, loop i from 1 to 2 setting dp[i]=dp[i-1]+1
Array dp=[5,10,0], loop i=2 setting dp[i]=dp[i-1]*2
examples.trace: Given dp=[5,10,0]. For i=2: access dp[1]=10, compute 10*2=20, store at dp[2] → dp becomes [5,10,20].

testCases.input: dp=[0,0,0], set dp[0]=2, loop i=1 to 2: dp[i]=dp[i-1]+3

approaches.name: DP with Optimized Space O(1)
approaches.summary: Use only two variables to store the last two DP values, updating iteratively to compute the Fibonacci sequence. This minimizes space usage while maintaining linear time.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Ways to Tile 2×N Using Optimized Space DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution using only two variables to compute domino tiling counts via Fibonacci sequence.
ladders.explanation: The domino tiling problem for a 2×N board asks how many ways we can cover the board completely with 2×1 dominoes (vertical) or 1×2 dominoes (horizontal, placed in pairs). The key insight is that the number of tilings follows the Fibonacci sequence: f(N) = f(N-1) + f(N-2), where f(0)=1 (empty board), f(1)=1 (one vertical domino). This recurrence arises because for the last column: if we place a vertical domino, we need to tile the remaining 2×(N-1) board; if we place two horizontal dominoes (stacked), we need to tile the remaining 2×(N-2) board. The optimized space approach uses only two variables to store the last two values, updating iteratively from base cases up to N, avoiding an entire DP array. Intuition: Since each state depends only on the previous two, we can 'slide' two variables forward. Step-by-step: Initialize prev2 = 1 (for N=0), prev1 = 1 (for N=1). For i from 2 to N: current = prev1 + prev2; update prev2 = prev1, prev1 = current. Return prev1. Time: O(N) from the loop. Space: O(1) for two variables. Connection: This is the complete implementation of the approach. Common mistakes: Forgetting base cases (N=0 should return 1), off-by-one errors in loop range, not handling small N separately. Real code pattern: def countWays(N): if N <= 1: return 1; a, b = 1, 1; for _ in range(2, N+1): a, b = b, a+b; return b.

examples.trace: Base: prev2=1 (f0), prev1=1 (f1). i=2: current=1+1=2; update prev2=1, prev1=2. i=3: current=2+1=3; update prev2=2, prev1=3. Loop ends, return prev1=3. Tilings: VVV, HH over cols 1-2 + V, V + HH over cols 2-3.
Base: prev2=1, prev1=1. i=2: current=2; prev2=1, prev1=2. i=3: current=3; prev2=2, prev1=3. i=4: current=5; prev2=3, prev1=5. i=5: current=8; prev2=5, prev1=8. Return 8.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Iterative Fibonacci Computation with Two Variables
ladders.desc: Compute the Nth Fibonacci number using only two variables in O(N) time and O(1) space.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the domino tiling solution, as the tiling count equals the (N+1)th Fibonacci number (with offset). The Fibonacci sequence is defined: F(0)=0, F(1)=1, F(n)=F(n-1)+F(n-2) for n≥2. Here, we compute F(N) iteratively without storing all previous values. Intuition: Since each Fibonacci number depends only on the two before it, we can maintain a 'window' of two variables that slide forward. This mimics the recurrence in constant space. Step-by-step: Initialize a=0 (F0), b=1 (F1). For i from 2 to N: compute c = a + b; update a = b, b = c. After loop, b holds F(N). For domino tiling, we use f(0)=1, f(1)=1, but the pattern is identical. Time: O(N) from loop. Space: O(1). Connection: This directly implements the DP recurrence for the full problem. Common mistakes: Incorrect base cases (e.g., starting with a=1, b=1 for standard Fibonacci), off-by-one in loop (should run N-1 times for F(N) if starting from F0,F1). Real code pattern: def fib(N): if N <= 1: return N; a, b = 0, 1; for _ in range(2, N+1): a, b = b, a+b; return b.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recurrence Relation for Domino Tilings: f(N) = f(N-1) + f(N-2)
ladders.desc: Derive and apply the recurrence that counts tilings by considering the last column.
ladders.explanation: The central insight for domino tiling is that the number of ways to tile a 2×N board, f(N), satisfies f(N) = f(N-1) + f(N-2) for N≥2, with base cases f(0)=1, f(1)=1. Intuition: Consider the rightmost column of the board. There are two possibilities: 1) Place a vertical domino covering that column, which uses up one column, leaving a 2×(N-1) board to tile—this contributes f(N-1) ways. 2) Place two horizontal dominoes stacked to cover the last two columns (since horizontal dominoes are 1×2, they span two columns in width), which uses up two columns, leaving a 2×(N-2) board to tile—this contributes f(N-2) ways. These are all possibilities because dominoes cannot overlap or leave gaps. The recurrence sums these disjoint cases. This is a classic DP recurrence similar to Fibonacci. Step-by-step: Define f(0)=1 (empty board counts as one way), f(1)=1 (one vertical domino). For N≥2, compute f(N) using the recurrence. Time: O(N) if computed iteratively. Space: O(1) with optimization. Connection: This recurrence is solved by the Fibonacci sub-routine. Common mistakes: Forgetting the base case f(0)=1, misinterpreting horizontal placement (requires two columns), not seeing disjointness. Real code pattern: Recurrence only, no full code: if N==0 or N==1: return 1; else return f(N-1) + f(N-2).

examples.trace: f(2) = f(1) + f(0) = 1 + 1 = 2. Ways: two vertical dominoes (VV) or two horizontal dominoes stacked (HH).
f(4) = f(3) + f(2). Need f(3)=f(2)+f(1)=2+1=3, f(2)=2. So f(4)=3+2=5. Tilings: Enumerate or use recurrence.

ladders.title: Building Block — Understanding Board Decomposition with Domino Placements
ladders.desc: Analyze how placing dominoes reduces the board size, leading to subproblems.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves breaking down the tiling problem into smaller subproblems by considering possible placements of dominoes. For a 2×N board, we focus on the right end. A vertical domino covers one column (2×1 area), reducing the problem to tiling a 2×(N-1) board. Two horizontal dominoes stacked cover two columns (each 1×2, together 2×2 area), reducing to a 2×(N-2) board. These are the only options because dominoes must align with the grid and cannot be placed diagonally or leave holes. Intuition: Each placement 'consumes' part of the board, creating a smaller instance of the same problem. This decomposition is recursive and leads to the recurrence. Step-by-step: Given board size N, list placements: vertical → subproblem size N-1; horizontal pair → subproblem size N-2. The total ways is sum of ways from each placement. Time/space: Analysis depends on implementation, but decomposition itself is O(1) per step. Connection: This decomposition directly gives the recurrence in level 2. Common mistakes: Considering single horizontal domino (invalid as it leaves a gap), missing the stacked requirement, not verifying placements cover the area completely. Real code pattern: Conceptual: def decompose(N): return [N-1, N-2] as subproblem sizes.

examples.trace: For 2×3 board: Option 1: Place vertical domino on right column → remaining board 2×2. Option 2: Place two horizontal dominoes stacked on right two columns → remaining board 2×1. So subproblems of sizes 2 and 1.
For 2×4 board: Vertical on right → 2×3 remaining. Horizontal pair on right two columns → 2×2 remaining. Sizes 3 and 2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Summing Two Numbers and Updating Variables in a Loop
ladders.desc: Perform iterative addition and variable swapping to compute a sequence.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves using a loop to repeatedly add two numbers and update variables, which is key to the optimized DP approach. Intuition: Many sequences (like Fibonacci) can be computed by maintaining a sliding window of the last two values. At each step, we compute the next value as the sum of the current two, then shift the window forward by updating variables. This avoids storing the entire sequence. Step-by-step: Start with two variables, say a and b, initialized to base values. For each iteration from 2 to N: compute sum = a + b; then set a = b and b = sum. After the loop, b holds the desired value. This operation is O(1) per step in time and uses O(1) space. Connection: This implements the iterative computation in level 1. Common mistakes: Incorrect update order (e.g., updating a before computing sum), off-by-one in loop count, not handling small N separately. Real code pattern: a, b = base1, base2; for i in range(2, N+1): next_val = a + b; a, b = b, next_val; return b.

examples.input: Compute third term with a=1, b=2, N=3
examples.trace: Start: a=1, b=2. i=2: sum=1+2=3; update a=2, b=3. i=3: sum=2+3=5; update a=3, b=5. Loop ends, b=5.

examples.input: Compute fifth term with a=0, b=1, N=5

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Introduction to Variables and Loops for Sequence Generation
ladders.desc: Use variables and a simple loop to generate a sequence of numbers step-by-step.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: using programming basics to create a sequence. Intuition: A sequence is a list of numbers where each number depends on previous ones. We can use variables to store current values and a loop to compute new ones iteratively. For example, to generate the first N numbers of a sequence where each term is the sum of the two before, we start with the first two terms, then repeatedly compute the next. This teaches how to model recurrences in code without recursion. Step-by-step: Initialize two variables with the first two sequence values. Use a for-loop that runs (N-2) times (since we already have two terms). In each iteration, compute the next term as the sum of the two variables, print or store it, then update the variables to shift forward. Time: O(N) for the loop. Space: O(1) for the variables. Connection: This basic operation is used in all higher levels. Common mistakes: Not initializing variables correctly, running the loop too many or too few times, not updating variables in the right order. Real code pattern: a = first_value; b = second_value; print a, b; for i in range(2, N): next = a + b; print next; a, b = b, next.

examples.input: Generate first 5 terms of sequence starting 1,1
examples.trace: Start: a=1, b=1. Print 1,1. i=2: next=1+1=2; print 2; update a=1,b=2. i=3: next=1+2=3; print 3; update a=2,b=3. i=4: next=2+3=5; print 5; update a=3,b=5. Stop after 5 terms.

examples.input: Generate first 4 terms of sequence starting 0,1
examples.trace: Start: a=0, b=1. Print 0,1. i=2: next=0+1=1; print 1; update a=1,b=1. i=3: next=1+1=2; print 2; update a=1,b=2. Stop after 4 terms.
